speaker: interviewee
text: --1995. The survivor is Clara Drier, D-R-I-E-R, and this is roll number three.

speaker: interviewer
text: Clara, you ought to tell us your first husband's name.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was [NON-ENGLISH] Harry, Harry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Harry?

speaker: interviewee
text: Cwangr, the last name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Spell it.

speaker: interviewee
text: C-W-A-N-G-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your son?

speaker: interviewee
text: My son is Paul Cwangr.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he kept the name, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: He keeps the name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have relatives in the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had, but I didn't know. I was so young. At home, I remember my mother used to talk about the relatives, but I didn't know--

speaker: interviewer
text: But you didn't know the name.

speaker: interviewee
text: --Even where to live. I didn't know the name, nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did you make arrangements to come to the US?

speaker: interviewee
text: His family actually find us. When we came to Germany, we were ready to leave to Israel. But one day, he looked in newspapers and saw an ad, like we're looking for him, my uncle. So we answered the ad, and he send us paper and he came to United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you have to wait from the time--

speaker: interviewee
text: Was not long.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not long?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not long.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he pay for your fare to come, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: We came without fare. I guess they didn't charge that time for the people. We came with the ship [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you sail from?

speaker: interviewee
text: From [NON-ENGLISH] from Germany, we came here [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember arriving in the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: We arrived. We had a terrible storm It was January 30 when we arrived.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was a storm as you arrived?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, as we were sailing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you remember the day you came into--

speaker: interviewee
text: United States?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: January 30, 1950.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me what it felt. How did you feel that day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I felt wonderful. The uncle was waiting for us. Then he brought us to Elizabeth, New Jersey. He gave us right away, apartment. And my husband was already sick for some time. And I went to work. I had a little child, but I had a woman used to take care of him.

speaker: interviewer
text: What work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing special, whatever I could get. I was working. They were making still for the army or the Navy, all kind of clothing, or jackets there. So I walked in for a job. They asked me, what do you do? I said nothing. He said, you got guts to come ask for a job. So I got a job. So I used to help him whatever, count, piece work, you know this.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay there doing that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed just 'til he died.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long?

speaker: interviewee
text: From 1951 'til '55.

speaker: interviewer
text: Quite a long time. And you had always a woman looking after the child?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH] had a woman, she was childless. So she used to love the little boy. So she offered me, if you want, I watch. I used to, but she didn't want to take him on by pay. Then sometime I had him bring to nurseries, but he didn't like. He was crying, so I'd rather have the woman taking care.

speaker: interviewer
text: And during this time, your husband's health wasn't--

speaker: interviewee
text: He used to get sick. He had a couple of heart attacks. We took him to a hospital and they couldn't find what's wrong with him. Then I went to a doctor's, cardiologist, and he right away say his heart is very bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: He wasn't very old then, was he?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he was 40s. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] It was 1955 when he died. So 1950, '55, you know, it was on and off to the hospitals. And one night, he just went to sleep, didn't wake up. Must have a heart attack in his sleep.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was your son then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Six years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't do anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean--

speaker: interviewee
text: I stopped working. I was taking care of my son, was going to a private school, to the [NON-ENGLISH] [? Elizabeth. ?] And I used to-- my husband left me business. He was sick. He couldn't go much to work. He used to work at the beginning in a factory, plastic factory, which he was not used to working at.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he start buying homes. He converted them to rooming houses. So when he died, I had two houses. I had a good income, I had free rent. And I collect Social Security for me and for my son. I was very comfortable. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then, I have to learn how to drive. [LAUGHTER] I bought a car, start driving. Then I met Saul. He met me. He met you? You didn't meet him? [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: Tell us. So we met two years later, I meant. So we have three children and four grandchildren. Married 37 years. I think it's OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- do you think you raised your children differently from Americans because of what you and Saul had suffered?

speaker: interviewee
text: I try.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what way was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Tried to sit and talk and explain. My one son, he knows. He went to Poland [INAUDIBLE] was very interested. And to Israel, he was living in Israel. He just came back. He's in love with Israel. But my two girls, not so-- my daughter was very interested, my grandchildren. So I try to explain, to talk to them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you raise them to be religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: I send them to Hebrew school. My son-- my son from Saul, my second husband, went to JEC, Jewish Education Center. He was bar mitzvah. I tried to raise him. Every holiday, we celebrate. We went to the synagogue, to the orthodox. I tried the best. But they know holidays. They know-- go to Shul once in a while.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have something you want to say to finish the tape?

speaker: interviewee
text: I just was thinking-- let me-- I want to say a few words. For me, it's important not only me, my children and my grandchildren, not to forget but for all future generation, never to forget. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is my first name is Clara, last name is Drier. Place was in Germany. Date of photo, I don't remember exactly. It was like in 1946, which was taken before we were coming to United States. This is my four children. The first one is Paul Cwanger. Paul Cwanger from the first marriage. The second one is Zachary Drier, my second marriage. The third one is Gina Drier and my youngest daughter Beth Drier. This is my two youngest grandchildren. First one is Jessica [NON-ENGLISH] The second one is my love [NON-ENGLISH]. I love them. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They live right now in Orlando, Florida. Date of photo, must be done last year. I don't know exactly the date. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is my all children. The firsts row on the top from the left to the right is my son Zachary Drier, Gina Drier, Beth Drier, and my granddaughter Lisa Cwanger. The second row from the left to the right is my son Paul Cwanger, his wife Sally Cwanger, and granddaughter Joanna Cwanger.
